course_meta.title: AI Engineering
phases: Phase 4 (Days 24–31): Probabilistic Reasoning, Fuzzy Logic & Neural Networks

roadmap.title: Foundations of AI & Syllabus Analysis

playlist_videos.title: Artificial Intelligence Syllabus Discussion and Analysis
What's the FUTURE of Artificial Intelligence in 2025

roadmap.watch_focus: Understand the core definition of AI and the roadmap for the course. Focus on how AI differs from traditional programming and the four categories of AI definitions (Thinking Humanly, Acting Humanly, Thinking Rationally, Acting Rationally).
roadmap.practice_task: Research and write a 200-word comparison between Weak AI and Strong AI, identifying three real-world examples for each currently in use.

quiz.q: Who is known as the father of Artificial Intelligence?
Which test is used to determine if a machine can demonstrate human-like intelligence?
What is the primary goal of AI?
options: To simulate human intelligence in machines
quiz.answer: To simulate human intelligence in machines

quiz.q: Acting Humanly approach is related to which of the following?
Which of these is a subset of AI?
What is 'Strong AI'?
The 'Laws of Thought' approach to AI is based on:
Which AI category focuses on 'Rational Agents'?
Which of the following is NOT a component of the Turing Test?
What year was the term 'Artificial Intelligence' officially coined?

roadmap.title: History, Applications, and AI Environments

playlist_videos.title: What is Artificial Intelligence | Learn AI with Real Life Examples | Can Machine Think??
What is State Space Search | Introduction to Problem Solving in Artificial Intelligence

roadmap.watch_focus: Learn the timeline of AI development, from the golden years to AI winters, and identify diverse sectors like Healthcare, Finance, and Robotics where AI is applied.
roadmap.practice_task: Identify a specific AI application in the finance sector (e.g., fraud detection) and map out the data inputs it likely requires and the decisions it outputs.

quiz.q: What does the term 'AI Winter' refer to?
options: Period of reduced funding and interest in AI
A specific AI algorithm for weather
quiz.answer: Period of reduced funding and interest in AI

quiz.q: Which of these was the first AI program?
AI in healthcare is primarily used for:
Expert Systems were prominent during which decade?
In which application is Computer Vision most critical?
The 'Dartmouth Workshop' is significant because:
options: The first robot was built there
It was the first AI winter

quiz.q: What does NLP stand for in AI?
Which company developed the 'Deep Blue' computer?
In Finance, AI is often used for 'Algorithmic Trading'. What is this?
options: Trading only in AI company stocks

quiz.q: Which AI application is used to suggest movies on Netflix?

roadmap.title: Intelligent Agents and PEAS Descriptors

playlist_videos.title: Uninformed Vs Informed Search in Artificial Intelligence with Example
Informed vs Uninformed vs Adversarial Search With Examples

roadmap.watch_focus: Understand the relationship between an agent, its sensors, actuators, and the environment. Master the PEAS (Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors) framework.
roadmap.practice_task: Choose three agents (e.g., Automated Taxi, Medical Diagnosis System, Part-picking Robot) and write down the full PEAS description for each.

quiz.q: What does an agent use to perceive its environment?
What does an agent use to act upon the environment?
In PEAS, what does the 'P' stand for?
A 'Percept' is defined as:
options: The agent's sensory input at any given instant
quiz.answer: The agent's sensory input at any given instant

quiz.q: For an automated taxi, the 'Environment' includes:
The 'S' in PEAS stands for:
Which of these is an Actuator for a robotic arm?
An agent that always chooses the action that maximizes its performance measure is called:
In a Medical Diagnosis system, what is the 'Performance Measure'?
Which of the following is NOT part of the PEAS framework?

roadmap.title: Properties of Environments

playlist_videos.title: Breadth First Search (BFS) with example | Uninformed Search

roadmap.watch_focus: Focus on the various dimensions of environments: Static vs. Dynamic, Discrete vs. Continuous, Observable vs. Partially Observable, etc.
roadmap.practice_task: Create a table classifying the environments for Chess, Poker, and Real-world Driving across all 6 environment dimensions discussed.

quiz.q: If an agent's sensors give it access to the complete state of the environment, it is:
An environment that does not change while the agent is deliberating is:
Chess is an example of which type of environment?
A 'Stochastic' environment is one where:
options: The next state is completely determined by the current state

quiz.q: Driving a car is a ________ environment.
If the environment is 'Episodic', the agent's current action:
options: Is determined by the first episode

quiz.q: Crossword puzzles are usually:
Which environment is most difficult for an AI to handle?
In a 'Multi-agent' environment:
A 'Deterministic' environment is one where:
options: The next state is perfectly predictable from current state and action
quiz.answer: The next state is perfectly predictable from current state and action

roadmap.title: Types of Agents

playlist_videos.title: Depth First Search (DFS) with example | Uninformed Search

roadmap.watch_focus: Distinguish between Simple Reflex, Model-based, Goal-based, and Utility-based agents. Understand why 'Learning Agents' are superior.
roadmap.practice_task: Draw a block diagram of a Utility-based Agent and explain the role of the 'Utility Function' in its decision-making process.

quiz.q: Which agent acts only on the basis of the current percept, ignoring history?
Which agent maintains an internal state to track parts of the world it can't see?
Goal-based agents differ from reflex agents by considering:
options: Future consequences to achieve a target
quiz.answer: Future consequences to achieve a target

quiz.q: Which agent uses a function to map a state to a real number describing 'happiness'?
What are the four components of a Learning Agent?
options: Learning element, Critic, Performance element, Problem generator
quiz.answer: Learning element, Critic, Performance element, Problem generator

quiz.q: The 'Critic' in a learning agent provides:
options: Feedback on how the agent is doing
quiz.answer: Feedback on how the agent is doing

quiz.q: The 'Problem Generator' is responsible for:
options: Suggesting actions that lead to new experiences
quiz.answer: Suggesting actions that lead to new experiences

quiz.q: A 'Simple Reflex Agent' works on which principle?
Which agent type is most suitable for a 'Partialy Observable' environment?
Which component of the learning agent is responsible for selecting external actions?

roadmap.title: Problem Solving & Search Basics

playlist_videos.title: Time & Space Complexity of BFS and DFS | Search space
Bidirectional Search Algorithm in Artificial Intelligence in Hindi with Real Life Examples

roadmap.watch_focus: Understand how to formulate a problem in AI using Initial State, Actions, Transition Model, Goal Test, and Path Cost. Visualize the State Space Search tree.
roadmap.practice_task: Formulate the '8-Puzzle' problem using the 5 components of problem formulation (States, Initial State, Actions, Transition Model, Goal Test).

quiz.q: What is the 'State Space' in AI search?
options: The physical memory of the computer
The set of all possible states reachable from the initial state
The path from start to finish
quiz.answer: The set of all possible states reachable from the initial state

quiz.q: Which of the following is NOT part of a well-defined problem?
In the Water Jug problem, what constitutes a 'State'?
options: The amount of water in each jug
quiz.answer: The amount of water in each jug

quiz.q: The process of removing a node from the 'frontier' and checking its successors is:
A 'Path' in a search tree is:
options: A sequence of states connected by actions
quiz.answer: A sequence of states connected by actions

quiz.q: What is 'Abstraction' in problem solving?
options: The process of removing irrelevant details from a problem
quiz.answer: The process of removing irrelevant details from a problem

quiz.q: The 'Frontier' (or Open List) contains:
options: Nodes that have been generated but not yet expanded
quiz.answer: Nodes that have been generated but not yet expanded

quiz.q: What is 'Path Cost'?
options: The time taken to find a solution
A numeric value assigned to a path
The number of nodes in the frontier
quiz.answer: A numeric value assigned to a path

quiz.q: Which of these is a classic AI toy problem?
What is the 'Optimal Solution'?
options: Any path that reaches the goal
The path with the lowest path cost among all solutions
The path that visits the most nodes
quiz.answer: The path with the lowest path cost among all solutions

roadmap.title: Best-First Search & Depth-Limited Search

playlist_videos.title: Best First Search Algorithm | How it Works | All Imp Points(Pros & Cons)
Depth-Limited Search (DLS) Explained | Uninformed Search in AI

roadmap.watch_focus: Compare Informed (Best-First) and Uninformed (DLS) search techniques. Focus on the use of evaluation functions to guide search.
roadmap.practice_task: Explain how DLS solves the infinite path problem of DFS and identify its main limitation compared to Iterative Deepening.

quiz.q: Best-First Search is a type of:
Which function is used by Best-First Search to choose the next node?
Depth-Limited Search (DLS) is used to overcome which drawback of DFS?
In DLS, what happens if the goal is deeper than the limit?
options: The algorithm fails to find it
The algorithm increases the limit automatically
quiz.answer: The algorithm fails to find it

quiz.q: Greedy Best-First Search expands the node that is closest to:
DLS is equivalent to DFS when the limit is set to:

DLS belongs to which search category?

roadmap.title: Iterative Deepening Search & 8-Puzzle Problem

playlist_videos.title: Iterative Deepening Depth-First Search (IDDFS) | Artificial Intelligence
8-Puzzle Problem in Artificial Intelligence without Heuristic | All Imp Points | Must Watch

roadmap.watch_focus: Understand why IDDFS combines the benefits of BFS (completeness) and DFS (space efficiency). Study the structure of the 8-puzzle as a state-space problem.
roadmap.practice_task: Calculate the state space size for an 8-puzzle and describe the blind search strategy used to reach the goal state.

quiz.q: IDDFS combines the benefits of which two algorithms?
What is the space complexity of IDDFS?
Is IDDFS complete if the branching factor is finite?
In an 8-puzzle problem, a 'state' is defined by:
IDDFS repeatedly applies which search with increasing limits?
What is the main advantage of IDDFS over BFS?
options: Always finds the goal in one step

quiz.q: In the 8-puzzle problem, what is the 'blank space' usually used for?
Why is IDDFS often preferred for large search spaces?
options: It manages memory efficiently and is complete
It never visits the same node twice
quiz.answer: It manages memory efficiently and is complete

quiz.q: The total number of states in an 8-puzzle is:
Which search strategy is used on Day 8 to solve the 8-puzzle?

roadmap.title: Heuristics & Informed Search for 8-Puzzle

playlist_videos.title: What is Heuristic in AI | Why we use Heuristic | How to Calculate Heuristic
How to Solve 8-Puzzle Problem with Heuristic(Informed Search) in Artificial Intelligence

roadmap.watch_focus: Master the concept of Heuristic Functions. Learn how to apply Manhattan Distance or Misplaced Tiles heuristics to solve puzzles efficiently.
roadmap.practice_task: Calculate the Manhattan Distance for a given 8-puzzle board configuration relative to a goal state.

quiz.q: A heuristic function h(n) estimates:
options: Cost from current node to goal
Cost from root to current node
quiz.answer: Cost from current node to goal

quiz.q: In the 8-puzzle, the 'Misplaced Tiles' heuristic counts:
options: Tiles not in their goal position
quiz.answer: Tiles not in their goal position

quiz.q: The 'Manhattan Distance' heuristic is calculated by:
options: Sum of horizontal and vertical distances
quiz.answer: Sum of horizontal and vertical distances

quiz.q: A heuristic is 'admissible' if it:
Why do we use heuristics in AI search?
options: To reduce the search space / time
To make the algorithm more complex
To find any solution, even if wrong
quiz.answer: To reduce the search space / time

quiz.q: Which heuristic is generally more 'informed' for the 8-puzzle?
In informed search, we pick the node with the ________ heuristic value.
Heuristic search is also known as:
Is 'Manhattan Distance' admissible for the 8-puzzle?
options: Only if there are no tiles
